agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics system with an established production domain model governing Shipment-to-Carrier assignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide heavy shipments (>500kg) into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing the existing supervisor-only reassignment control.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be delivered, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for transporting a Shipment. Assigned 1:1 to a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The locked binding of exactly one Carrier to exactly one Shipment. Only a Supervisor may change this binding once set."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment leaves for delivery. The system guarantees every Shipment has exactly one Carrier at this moment."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed feature to automatically decompose a Shipment exceeding 500kg into multiple sub-shipments with independent Carrier assignments, without Supervisor approval."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and state integrity constraint. A Shipment with zero or more than one Carrier is in a corrupt state — accountability, tracking, billing, and liability all depend on this 1:1 relationship.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'delivered', COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization and state integrity constraint. It prevents unauthorized mutation of a locked binding, which would break chain-of-custody, liability tracking, and audit trails.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change Carrier assignment without Supervisor authorization must be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — a shipment leaves on exactly one truck with exactly one responsible party. Violating this means no one is accountable for the freight in transit.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment weighing exactly 500kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Boundary must be explicitly defined (>500 vs >=500). Cannot ship this feature with ambiguity on the threshold."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is already assigned to a Carrier and then its weight is updated to exceed 500kg.","expected_behavior":"Splitting after assignment would require reassignment of the original Carrier, which triggers the Supervisor-only invariant (BR-2). The feature as described would violate this."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500kg after splitting.","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting? The feature request is silent on this. Could produce unbounded decomposition."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment's Carrier fails or cancels. Who is accountable for the parent Shipment?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The current model has clear single-Carrier accountability. Splitting fragments this accountability with no described escalation path."},{"scenario":"Billing and invoicing: does the customer pay one rate for the original Shipment or multiple rates for sub-shipments?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Financial model for split shipments is not addressed."},{"scenario":"Tracking: customer expects one tracking number per Shipment. Sub-shipments create multiple.","expected_behavior":"Customer-facing tracking experience is undefined for split shipments."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Significant. Splitting shipments across multiple carriers changes the billing model, may increase total shipping cost (multiple pickups, multiple carriers), and introduces complex reconciliation. No financial model was provided with the request.","operational":"High risk. Introduces a new 'sub-shipment' aggregate that does not exist in the current domain. Every downstream system — tracking, billing, dispatch, reporting, SLA monitoring — must understand parent/child Shipment relationships. This is not a feature; it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customer confusion is likely. Customers currently expect one shipment = one tracking number = one carrier. Splitting silently behind the scenes breaks this mental model without a described communication strategy.","risk":"CRITICAL. The feature as described directly violates two production invariants (BR-1 and BR-2). Shipping this without resolving the contradictions would corrupt the domain model that the rest of the system relies on for correctness.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must now coordinate multi-carrier pickups for what was previously a single shipment. Dispatch workflow fundamentally changes."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier reassignment is being bypassed for auto-split scenarios. This undermines the control structure they rely on."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Now receive partial shipments. Carrier contracts, SLAs, and rate cards may not support partial loads or may price them differently."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive multiple deliveries for what they booked as one shipment. Tracking, communication, and delivery expectations all change."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Must reconcile multiple carrier invoices against a single customer order. Revenue recognition and cost allocation become complex."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Every system that touches Shipment must now understand the parent/child relationship. This is a cross-cutting domain model change, not an isolated feature."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The feature request directly contradicts two established production invariants. The spec provides no resolution strategy for these contradictions, no financial model, no definition of the sub-shipment lifecycle, and no stakeholder communication plan. Confidence is low because the request as written cannot be implemented without either violating the existing domain model or fundamentally redefining it — and the request does not acknowledge this.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradictions between the feature request and existing invariants are clear and unambiguous","The 1:1 Carrier-Shipment invariant is well-established and load-bearing across the system"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the existing invariants or introduce a new aggregate type","The financial and billing model for split shipments","The customer-facing experience for split shipments","Whether 'sub-shipment' is a new aggregate root or a child entity of Shipment"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model as described is accurate and in production","The 500kg threshold is a business policy, not a physical constraint","The product team has not yet consulted with Supervisors, Finance, or Carrier partners about this change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment). If a Shipment is split into sub-shipments each with a different Carrier, either: (A) the parent Shipment no longer has exactly one Carrier, violating the invariant, or (B) 'sub-shipments' are fully independent Shipments, in which case the original Shipment must be canceled/closed, which has its own implications. The feature request does not acknowledge or resolve this conflict.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Engineering","Customers","Finance"],"options":["A: Introduce a new 'ShipmentGroup' aggregate that contains multiple child Shipments, each preserving the 1:1 Carrier invariant. The parent becomes a grouping concept, not a Shipment.","B: Relax the 1:1 Carrier invariant to allow multi-carrier Shipments (DANGEROUS — breaks every downstream system).","C: Reject the feature and instead surface a recommendation to the Supervisor to manually split, preserving the existing control structure."],"recommendation":"Option A. Introduce a ShipmentGroup aggregate. Each sub-shipment is a first-class Shipment with its own Carrier, preserving all existing invariants. The ShipmentGroup provides the parent-child traceability. This requires explicit domain modeling before any implementation begins."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature bypasses Supervisor authority for carrier assignment (BR-2). The existing model explicitly requires Supervisor involvement for any reassignment. Auto-splitting assigns new Carriers to new sub-shipments without Supervisor approval, which directly contradicts the established authorization model.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Operations","Compliance/Audit"],"options":["A: Require Supervisor approval for auto-split (defeats the 'automatic' purpose but preserves the control structure).","B: Define auto-split as a 'new assignment' rather than a 'reassignment', arguing the sub-shipments are new entities that never had a prior Carrier. This is semantically defensible only if sub-shipments are first-class Shipments.","C: Create a new 'System' actor with Supervisor-equivalent authority for automated operations, with full audit trail."],"recommendation":"Option B combined with Option A's ShipmentGroup approach. If sub-shipments are new Shipment aggregates, their initial Carrier assignment is not a 'reassignment' and does not require Supervisor override. However, the original Shipment's Carrier IS being effectively removed, which still triggers BR-2. A Supervisor approval step — even if streamlined — should be required to close/cancel the original Shipment's Carrier assignment."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The feature request provides no lifecycle definition for sub-shipments. Critical questions are unanswered: What happens if one sub-shipment fails? Is the parent Shipment 'delivered' only when all sub-shipments are delivered? Can sub-shipments be independently canceled? What is the SLA — per sub-shipment or for the whole group?","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Operations","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["A: Define sub-shipment lifecycle as fully independent (simplest, but customer sees fragmented delivery).","B: Define sub-shipment lifecycle as coordinated under the parent (complex, but preserves customer experience)."],"recommendation":"This must be defined by Product and Operations together before any implementation. Engineering cannot infer the correct lifecycle."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Feature as specified requires a single Shipment to have multiple Carriers (one per sub-shipment), directly violating the 1:1 Carrier-Shipment invariant.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Feature bypasses Supervisor authority by automatically assigning Carriers to sub-shipments without Supervisor involvement.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it."},{"violation":"At dispatch time, the original Shipment would either have no Carrier (split away) or multiple (via sub-shipments), violating the dispatch guarantee.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier."}]}
